aliases: Indian Flatbread with Egg and Onion

english: Indian Flatbread with Egg and Onion

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Singapore roti prata with egg and onion. Mapped to the closest parent class 'flatbread food product' (FOODON_03315091) as an approximate match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Flat bread) used as the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for Indian-style layered prata with egg and onion filling in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for roti prata or Singapore Indian flatbread with egg and onion. SNOMED CT does not have a specific concept for this dish.

serving.description: 1 piece of prata with egg and onion
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one piece of egg and onion prata (approximately 150 g), as commonly served at Singapore hawker centres and mamak stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Prata (egg, onion) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95. Micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) carried from input per_100g_meta at confidence 0.70; these are treated as supporting estimates and documented accordingly. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB for this entry and set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Roti / Indian Flatbread — analogous estimate based on white wheat flour flatbread (chapati/roti, GI ~70) from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore roti prata with egg and onion in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 70 based on the closest analogous food: white wheat flour flatbread / roti (GI ~70, University of Sydney GI Database). The egg and onion filling does not materially alter the GI of the wheat-flour base. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: (70 x 22.68 x 1.50) / 100 = 23.8, rounded to 23.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 275 mg/100 g with low potassium and low dietary fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 275 mg/100 g is in the borderline range; individuals with hypertension should limit to one piece per meal, avoid adding salt at the table, and consume any curry dipping sauce sparingly to keep total sodium within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~70) refined wheat flour base with low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The refined wheat flour base produces rapid postprandial glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one piece per meal, pair with a non-starchy vegetable or protein side dish, and monitor blood glucose response carefully.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 266.67 mg/100 g from egg and saturated fat 4.0 g/100 g from ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high cholesterol content from egg and saturated fat from ghee are relevant for cardiovascular risk; individuals with CVD should limit frequency of consumption, request vegetable oil substitution for ghee where possible, and ensure this dish is part of an overall diet low in saturated fat and dietary cholesterol.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prata with Egg and Onion (roti prata telur bawang) is a popular Singaporean Indian flatbread made from a layered, unleavened wheat flour dough enriched with ghee, cooked on a hot iron griddle until crispy on the outside and soft and flaky on the inside. The dough is stretched and folded repeatedly to create its characteristic laminated layers, then an egg is cracked onto the dough and diced onion is scattered over it before the prata is folded and pressed flat on the hot plate. The result is a golden-brown, slightly crispy flatbread with a savoury egg and onion filling. It is a staple breakfast and supper dish at Singapore hawker centres and mamak (Muslim Indian) stalls, typically served with fish or chicken curry for dipping. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 220 kcal, 8.16 g protein, 10.76 g fat (of which 4.0 g saturated from ghee), and 22.68 g carbohydrate with only 1.0 g dietary fibre, reflecting its refined wheat flour base. Sodium is 275 mg/100 g and cholesterol is notably high at 266.67 mg/100 g due to the egg content. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 70 (high), making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk. It is halal-certified in most hawker settings and is not suitable for vegans due to egg and ghee content.
common_user_queries: how many calories in egg onion prata

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact match to this dish. Micronutrients carried from input at 0.70 confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest approved equivalent (white wheat flour flatbread, GI ~70).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Singapore prata preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. Micronutrients calcium (46.67 mg), iron (1.67 mg), cholesterol (266.67 mg), saturated_fat (4.0 g), trans_fat (0.07 g), and vitamin_c (2.0 mg) are retained from input at confidence 0.70 (originally attributed to a delivery platform source in the input; treated here as supporting estimates only and not as primary approved-source values — flagged for future verification against ICMR-NIN or USDA). GI of 70 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement for Singapore egg-onion prata is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026.
